## Patient Initial Report

**Patient Details**
- Age: 42-year-old
- Sex: Male

**Presenting Complaint**
- Polyuria
- Polydipsia
- Blurred vision
- Fatigue

**History**
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes

**Vital Signs**
- Blood pressure: 140/90 mmHg

**Laboratory Values**
- Blood sugar: 350 mg/dl
- HbA1c: 10.5%

## Prelim Diagnosis Report

### 1. Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Syndrome (HHS)
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A serious complication of type 2 diabetes characterized by severe hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, and dehydration.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Patient presents with polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, fatigue, elevated blood sugar (350 mg/dl), and HbA1c (10.5%).
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes suggests poorly controlled diabetes and potential HHS.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Hospitalization for intravenous fluids.
- Insulin therapy.
- Electrolyte monitoring and replacement.

### 2. Diabetic Retinopathy
**📌 Disease Description:**
- Damage to the blood vessels in the retina due to chronic high blood sugar levels.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Patient reports blurred vision.
- Long history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes increases risk of diabetic retinopathy.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Ophthalmological examination.
- Fundoscopy.
- Optical coherence tomography (OCT).

### 3. Diabetic Nephropathy
**📌 Disease Description:**
- Kidney damage resulting from prolonged high blood sugar levels.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- History of type 2 diabetes and elevated blood pressure (140/90 mmHg) are risk factors.
- Further investigation is warranted to assess kidney function.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio.
- Serum creatinine and eGFR.
- Blood pressure control.## Best Practices Report

### **🩺 Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS)** [10 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/419](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/419)
  - **📖 Summary:** PMS is characterized by repetitive, cyclical, physical, and behavioral symptoms during the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, extending into the first few days of menses. Symptoms must not be exacerbations of other disorders and must interfere with the woman's life.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.acog.org/news/news-articles/2023/11/acog-releases-new-guidelines-on-management-of-premenstrual-disorders](https://www.acog.org/news/news-articles/2023/11/acog-releases-new-guidelines-on-management-of-premenstrual-disorders)
  - **📖 Summary:** ACOG's guidelines recommend evidence-based treatments for PMS and PMDD, including SSRIs, combined hormonal oral contraceptives, CBT, acupuncture, exercise, nutritional therapies, patient education, and self-help strategies.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.uptodate.com/contents/treatment-of-premenstrual-syndrome-and-premenstrual-dysphoric-disorder](https://www.uptodate.com/contents/treatment-of-premenstrual-syndrome-and-premenstrual-dysphoric-disorder)
  - **📖 Summary:** PMS involves physical and behavioral symptoms that occur repetitively in the second half of the menstrual cycle, interfering with daily life. Charting mood and physical symptoms daily helps in diagnosis.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.pharmacytimes.com/view/clinical-overview-treatment-options-for-premenstrual-syndrome-pms-](https://www.pharmacytimes.com/view/clinical-overview-treatment-options-for-premenstrual-syndrome-pms-)
  - **📖 Summary:** 20-40% of individuals experience PMS, characterized by emotional, behavioral, and physical symptoms appearing at the end of the luteal phase and resolving with the start of menstruation. 2-8% suffer from PMDD.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/premenstrual-syndrome/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20376787](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/premenstrual-syndrome/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20376787)
  - **📖 Summary:** Doctors may attribute symptoms to PMS if they are part of a predictable premenstrual pattern. Recording signs and symptoms for at least two menstrual cycles can help establish this pattern. Management includes lifestyle changes and medication.

### **🩺 Dysmenorrhea** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://thekingsleyclinic.com/resources/understanding-dysmenorrhea-causes-symptoms-and-pain-relief/](https://thekingsleyclinic.com/resources/understanding-dysmenorrhea-causes-symptoms-and-pain-relief/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Managing dysmenorrhea effectively involves medicaltreatments, lifestyle adjustments, and self-care. Symptom diaries help identify triggers and evaluate the effectiveness of remedies.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/253812-treatment](https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/253812-treatment)
  - **📖 Summary:** Many women self-medicate for dysmenorrhea with analgesics, NSAIDs, and heat application.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.rupahealth.com/post/how-to-manage-dysmenorrhea-expert-advice-on-menstrual-pain](https://www.rupahealth.com/post/how-to-manage-dysmenorrhea-expert-advice-on-menstrual-pain)
  - **📖 Summary:** Fish oil may benefit dysmenorrhea due to its anti-inflammatory effects, while caffeine may worsen it. Medical interventions may be recommended based on the patient's situation.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/4148-dysmenorrhea](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/4148-dysmenorrhea)
  - **📖 Summary:** Dysmenorrhea is painful menstruation. Primary dysmenorrhea involves recurring menstrual cramps not due to other conditions. Secondary dysmenorrhea is caused by a condition or infection in the reproductive organs. Contact a healthcare provider if painful periods interfere with normal tasks.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2021/0800/p164.html](https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2021/0800/p164.html)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hormonal contraceptives like patches, rings, progestin implants, medroxyprogesterone injections, and levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs are acceptable for managing dysmenorrhea. Levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs are as effective as or superior to oral contraceptives for primary and endometriosis-related secondary dysmenorrhea.

### **🩺 Costochondritis** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.lifelongwellness.org/wellness-academy/health/how-i-cured-my-costochondritis/](https://www.lifelongwellness.org/wellness-academy/health/how-i-cured-my-costochondritis/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Acupuncture may provide relief for costochondritis by stimulating specific points, promoting energy flow, and reducing pain.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://wellwisp.com/costochondritis-how-to-treat/](https://wellwisp.com/costochondritis-how-to-treat/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diagnosing costochondritis involves a medical history and physical examination to rule out other causes of chest discomfort. Mindfulness practices can also be helpful.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/22167-costochondritis](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/22167-costochondritis)
  - **📖 Summary:** Resting the chest and ribcage is the most common treatment. OTC medications like NSAIDs or acetaminophen can relieve pain.- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/300](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/300)
  - **📖 Summary:** Costochondritis presents with anterior chest-wall pain exacerbated by chest movements and deep inspiration. A key sign is pain on palpation of costochondral joints. Clinical diagnosis is essential, and tests are used to rule out other conditions.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://healthprep.com/articles/conditions/treat-costochondritis/](https://healthprep.com/articles/conditions/treat-costochondritis/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Treatment often doesn't require medical intervention, but medications or surgery may be needed for severe cases. Patients should avoid triggering exercises.

### **🩺 Major Depressive Disorder** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://primeinc.org/online/improving-major-depressive-disorder-care-updated-approaches-therapeutic-options](https://primeinc.org/online/improving-major-depressive-disorder-care-updated-approaches-therapeutic-options)
  - **📖 Summary:** Advances in MDD treatment are shifting care approaches. Understanding evolving best practices and the latest clinical data is crucial, especially for treatment-resistant depression.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://adaa.org/resources-professionals/practice-guidelines-mdd](https://adaa.org/resources-professionals/practice-guidelines-mdd)
  -**📖 Summary:** MDD is a prevalent and costly condition, with a lifetime prevalence of about 16% among U.S. adults. Women are 1.5 to 3 times more likely to experience it.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.healthquality.va.gov/guidelines/MH/mdd/](https://www.healthquality.va.gov/guidelines/MH/mdd/)
  - **📖 Summary:** The guideline describes critical decision points in MDD management and provides evidence-based recommendations for practitioners in the DOD and VA Health Care systems.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/depression/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20356013](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/depression/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20356013)
  - **📖 Summary:** Mental health professionals may use DSM-5 criteria for diagnosing depression. Psychotherapy involves discussing the condition with a mental health professional.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.healthquality.va.gov/guidelines/MH/mdd/MDDCPGClinicianSummaryFINAL1.pdf](https://www.healthquality.va.gov/guidelines/MH/mdd/MDDCPGClinicianSummaryFINAL1.pdf)
  - **📖 Summary:** MDD presents with depressed mood, loss of interest, decreased energy, guilt, disturbed sleep or appetite, and poor concentration. It is the most prevalent and disabling form of depression.

### **🩺 Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Syndrome (HHS)** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/1011](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/1011)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, weight loss, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, and hypotension, and in severe cases, shock. It is characterized by profound hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL), hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), and volume depletion without significant ketoacidosis. It may be the first presentation of type 2 diabetes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/](https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a medical emergency involving serum hyperosmolality, hyperglycemia, and dehydration without ketoacidosis, resulting from a relative lack of insulin. Many children with type 2 diabetes and HHS may only temporarily require insulin treatment.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.diabetes.org.uk/sites/default/files/2022-02/JBDS+06+The+Management+of+Hyperosmolar+Hyperglycaemic+State+(HHS)+in+Adults+FINAL.pdf](https://www.diabetes.org.uk/sites/default/files/2022-02/JBDS+06+The+Management+of+Hyperosmolar+Hyperglycaemic+State+(HHS)+in+Adults+FINAL.pdf)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a medical emergency that occurs less frequently than DKA.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482142/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482142/)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a complication of diabetes mellitus with a high mortality rate. It is defined by plasma glucose >600 mg/dL, plasma effective osmolarity >320 mOsm/L, and absence of significant ketoacidosis.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a serious complication of diabetes with very high blood sugar levels for a long period, leading to severe dehydration and confusion. Stopping diabetes medications can lead to HHS. A main complication of treatment is a rapid drop in blood sugar.

### **🩺 Diabetic Retinopathy** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.aaojournal.org/article/S0161-6420(24)00784-X/fulltext](https://www.aaojournal.org/article/S0161-6420(24)00784-X/fulltext)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetic Retinopathy Preferred Practice Pattern® by the American Academy of Ophthalmology.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/532](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/532)
  - **📖 Summary:** Sight-threatening signs include macular edema, retinal or optic disk new vessels, and vitreous hemorrhage.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/diabetic-retinopathy/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20371617](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/diabetic-retinopathy/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20371617)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetic retinopathy is best diagnosed with a comprehensive dilated eye exam. Prompt treatment is needed for proliferative diabetic retinopathy or macular edema, often involving focal laser treatment. Regular eye exams are essential post-treatment.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.webmd.com/diabetes/retinopathy-causes-treatments](https://www.webmd.com/diabetes/retinopathy-causes-treatments)
  - **📖 Summary:** Routine screening is crucial for early detection. People with type 1 diabetes should have an eye exam within 5 years of diagnosis, and those with type 2 should be screened earlier.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.asrs.org/patients/retinal-diseases/3/diabetic-retinopathy](https://www.asrs.org/patients/retinal-diseases/3/diabetic-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** Leaking blood vessels cause diabetic macular edema, leading to swelling in the macula. New blood vessels or scar tissue on the retina indicate proliferative diabetic retinopathy. Retina specialists can assist with rehabilitation for irreversible vision loss.

### **🩺 Diabetic Nephropathy** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24218418/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24218418/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Aggressive glucose and blood pressure control may not be best for every patient. Optimizing RAAS blockade via sodium restriction and/or diuretics is advocated.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/238946-treatment](https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/238946-treatment)
  - **📖 Summary:** Key issues include glycemic control, hypertension management, dietary salt reduction, and, in advanced cases, phosphorus and potassium restriction. Salt restriction significantly lowers blood pressure.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/diabetic-nephropathy/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20354562](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/diabetic-nephropathy/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20354562)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetic nephropathy is diagnosed during regular diabetes management testing. Annual testing is recommended for those with type 2 diabetes or type 1 diabetes for more than five years. Kidney or kidney-pancreas transplants are the best treatment for kidney failure.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/530](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/530)
  - **📖 Summary:** DKD is usually a clinical diagnosis in patients with long-standing diabetes (>10 years) with albuminuria and/or reduced eGFR, without other primary causes of kidney damage. It is defined by albuminuria (≥30 mg/g) and progressive reduction in eGFR.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4171795/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4171795/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Recommendations include yearly albumin to creatinine ratio checks, optimizing glucose control (HbA1c <7%), ACE inhibitors or ARBs as first-line treatment, managing blood pressure (<140/90 mmHg), and statin therapy for patients <50 years old with CKD and diabetes and all patients with CKD >50 years old regardless of diabetes.
